Economists are also studying how people are interacting with one another. For example, they are examining how the multitude of buyers and sellers of a good are interacting to determine the price the good is sold at and the quantity is sold. Economists are using the term opportunity cost to describe what you give up to get something. Because people are facing trade-offs, making decisions is requiring comparing the costs and benefits of alternative courses of action. Rational people are systematically and purposefully doing the best they can to achieve their objectives, given the available opportunities. Economists are assuming that people are rational and are responding to incentives. One important insight of economics is that incentives are mattering. When the price of a good is rising, buyers are tending to buy less and sellers are tending to sell more.

Public policy is able to change the costs or benefits people are facing and, as a result, is altering their behavior.
